narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and middle-family income background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including your full-time study plans and school decile rankings of 6th in Year 1 and 2nd in Year 3. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 65% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your LSAT score of 21.30, which is notably below the typical applicant's 36.70 and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Your undergraduate GPA, right around the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by pulling the odds slightly toward passing success. Similarly, your lower Year 3 decile ranking compared to the average nudged things a bit toward failure risk, while your stronger Year 1 decile and middle-income family status offered modest positive influences. Being a full-time student, like most applicants, provided a small boost too. Overall, your profile stands out from typical students mainly due to that lower LSAT, which overshadowed your otherwise average-to-solid academic standing. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income home—many in similar situations improve with targeted prep. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.70 average, as that single factor carries the most weight in these predictions. Strengthening your Year 3 performance or GPA through additional coursework could also help balance things out. Consider free or low-cost LSAT resources tailored for students like you, and reapply once you've boosted that key score—admissions models reward clear improvements. You've got the foundation; a focused push on your LSAT could turn this around and get you into law school. Keep pursuing your goals with confidence.
